quick_facts.label: Position and cadre
quick_facts.value: Assistant Commandant (General Duty) in ITBP is classified in the published recruitment rules as a General Central Service, Group ‘A’, Gazetted, non-ministerial post.

quick_facts.label: Primary external entry route
quick_facts.value: For the general-duty post, the rules provide for 50% of vacancies to be filled by direct recruitment through the all-India competitive examination conducted by UPSC; the UPSC CAPF (ACs) process allocates a force based on merit position and the candidate’s stated force preferences.

quick_facts.label: Education requirement
quick_facts.value: The published ITBP General Duty Group A recruitment rules require a bachelor’s degree from a recognised university or an equivalent qualification. The UPSC CAPF (ACs) 2025 notice likewise required a bachelor’s degree.

quick_facts.label: Selection process
quick_facts.value: In the UPSC CAPF (ACs) 2025 cycle, selection comprised a written examination, physical standards/physical efficiency tests, interview/personality test, and medical standards tests. Final merit was based on the written examination and interview/personality-test marks, subject to medical fitness.

quick_facts.label: Pay context
quick_facts.value: The 2018 ITBP General Duty Group A recruitment rules list Assistant Commandant at Pay Matrix Level 10, ₹56,100–₹1,77,500. This is the published basic-pay matrix range, not a guaranteed take-home amount; applicable allowances and future pay-policy changes can alter total remuneration.

quick_facts.label: Service environment
quick_facts.value: ITBP is a border-guarding force trained for high-altitude mountainous terrain. MHA’s ITBP profile states that some border outposts are at heights up to 18,750 feet, where temperatures can fall to minus 40°C.

sections.heading: Responsibilities and operational role
paragraphs: An Assistant Commandant is an officer rank in the ITBP statutory rank structure. The ITBP Act specifically provides that an Assistant Commandant commanding a company, detachment or outpost has defined authority to proceed against personnel charged with offences and to award specified minor punishments, subject to the Act. In practice, this places command, discipline, supervision and accountable decision-making at the centre of the role.

ITBP’s published role includes constant border vigilance, border management, protection of border check posts and populations, counter-insurgency and internal-security duties, protection of vital installations and vulnerable points, disaster management, civic action, and a wartime role with the Army. An Assistant Commandant’s exact assignment depends on posting and formation, but these force-level duties frame the operational work that officers may lead or support.
bullets: Command and supervise a company, detachment or outpost when assigned.
Maintain discipline, readiness, reporting and lawful exercise of delegated command powers.
Lead or support border-management, security, internal-security and disaster-response tasks according to deployment.

sections.heading: Entry through UPSC CAPF (ACs)
paragraphs: For an applicant seeking this canonical General Duty officer role from outside ITBP, the relevant route is the UPSC Central Armed Police Forces (Assistant Commandants) Examination. The ITBP General Duty recruitment rules prescribe direct recruitment through the UPSC all-India competitive examination for 50% of vacancies, while the UPSC notice states that service/force allocation is determined by merit position and candidates’ force preferences. Selecting ITBP as a preference therefore does not itself guarantee ITBP allocation.

Eligibility and dates must be checked in the notice for the particular examination year. As a dated example, the 2025 UPSC CAPF (ACs) notice set the general age band at 20–25 years on August 1, 2025, required a bachelor’s degree, and allowed both male and female candidates to be appointed. The same notice set out category and service-related age relaxations; applicants should use the live UPSC notice rather than relying on a previous cycle.

The 2025 selection scheme used two written papers: General Ability and Intelligence (250 marks) and General Studies, Essay and Comprehension (200 marks). Written-qualified candidates proceeded to physical standards and physical-efficiency testing, then interview/personality testing and medical standards testing. The interview carried 150 marks; final merit was drawn from the written examination and interview, with appointment dependent on satisfying medical standards.
bullets: Earn a recognised bachelor’s degree or equivalent qualification.
Apply in the relevant annual UPSC CAPF (ACs) notification and indicate force preferences carefully.
Prepare for the written papers, physical standards/efficiency requirements, interview and medical examination.
Treat age limits, vacancy numbers, application dates, standards and force-allocation rules as cycle-specific.

sections.heading: Skills to build
paragraphs: The examination and the force’s operational environment point to a blend of academic, communication, physical and leadership capabilities. UPSC’s written syllabus tests reasoning, quantitative ability, data interpretation, general science, current affairs, essay writing and English-language comprehension/communication. The physical tests and medical requirements mean candidates also need sustained conditioning and careful attention to fitness.

The role’s command and high-altitude border-security context make calm judgement, team leadership, situational awareness, discipline, clear written and spoken communication, and resilience especially relevant. These are role-aligned capabilities inferred from the statutory command responsibilities of Assistant Commandants and ITBP’s published duties; they are not a separate exhaustive eligibility checklist.
bullets: Logical reasoning, quantitative aptitude, general awareness and clear analytical writing.
Hindi/English examination readiness, including English precis, comprehension and communication components specified by UPSC.
Running, speed, jumping, strength and injury-aware physical conditioning for the prescribed tests.
Leadership, operational judgement, discipline and communication for command assignments.
Resilience and adaptability for remote, high-altitude and security-sensitive deployments.

sections.heading: Workplace and deployment context
paragraphs: ITBP is assigned border-guarding responsibilities on the India–China border and is specially trained to operate in high-altitude mountain terrain. Its work can include border outposts, units and operational deployments rather than a conventional office-only environment. MHA describes ITBP tasks that also extend to internal security, counter-insurgency, vital-installation and VIP security, disaster management, civic action and other duties.

The published recruitment rules identify Assistant Commandant as a Group A gazetted general-duty post, and the ITBP Act places the rank within the force’s officer structure. Posting, field exposure, command appointment and day-to-day work will vary materially by unit, location, operational requirements and career stage.
bullets: Potential field and border-outpost service in remote, mountainous areas.
Operational work may involve irregular hours, demanding weather and mobility constraints.
Assignments may also arise in security, internal-security, disaster-management or administrative/training contexts.

sections.heading: Career progression
paragraphs: The statutory officer hierarchy lists Assistant Commandant below Deputy Commandant, Second-in-Command and Commandant, followed by senior ranks including Deputy Inspector-General, Inspector-General, Additional Director-General and Director-General. Advancement is governed by the applicable recruitment rules, service record, vacancy, eligibility conditions and prescribed training rather than being automatic.

Under the published 2018 General Duty Group A recruitment rules, promotion from Assistant Commandant to Deputy Commandant requires five years of regular service in Level 10, including two years’ experience as Company Commander of a service company in a service battalion, plus successful completion of the prescribed pre-promotional training course; the rules specify an exception to that company-commander condition for officers in the central sports team. The same rules list Deputy Commandant at Level 11, Second-in-Command at Level 12 and Commandant at Level 13. Requirements can be amended, so serving personnel should consult current ITBP/MHA orders.
bullets: Entry rank: Assistant Commandant (General Duty), Level 10.
Next published rank: Deputy Commandant (General Duty), Level 11.
Subsequent published ranks include Second-in-Command (Level 12) and Commandant (Level 13).
Promotion is conditional on qualifying service, required experience where applicable, prescribed training and selection processes.

sections.heading: Compensation context
paragraphs: The ITBP General Duty Group A recruitment rules published in 2018 place Assistant Commandant at Level 10 of the Central Government pay matrix: ₹56,100–₹1,77,500. This describes the basic pay range in that rule, not a single monthly in-hand salary.

Total compensation is variable. It can depend on basic-pay stage, admissible central-government and force allowances, place of posting, deductions, pension arrangements and changes to government policy. ITBP recruitment material for another Group A Assistant Commandant cadre describes Level 10 pay plus admissible ITBP/MHA allowances and coverage under the government’s contributory pension arrangement; applicants should confirm the terms in the notification and appointment conditions applicable to their own recruitment cycle and cadre.
bullets: Published basic-pay context: Level 10, ₹56,100–₹1,77,500.
Do not treat online ‘in-hand salary’ estimates as official or universal.
Confirm current allowances, deductions and pension terms from the relevant official notification and appointment documents.
